metadata.title: The Fire in the Fan: Teaching Rebellion Through Song

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The aim is not merely to teach facts about Jane Austen. The real goal is to rewire how people understand what rebellion actually looks like. Both things stay true at once: Austen as literature and as a model of quiet defiance. The song makes that threat something you can feel rather than something merely explained.

artifactLines: The aim is not merely to teach facts about Jane Austen.
The real goal is to rewire how people understand what rebellion actually looks like.
Both things stay true at once: Austen as literature and as a model of quiet defiance.
The song makes that threat something you can feel rather than something merely explained.

beats.narration_text: The goal that struck me wasn't teaching facts about Jane Austen but rewiring how people understand what rebellion looks like, and that both things can be true at once. I want to build a lesson around that. Help me: (1) identify the quiet, coded forms of rebellion in Austen that a song could surface; (2) design lyrics that carry the felt threat rather than stating it; (3) plan how to measure whether a listener's idea of rebellion actually shifted afterward.

output: The song is two minutes and thirty-seven seconds long
The goal is not just to teach facts about Jane Austen
None of this explains why Jane Austen is taught
